skill.name: dbt-transformation-patterns
skill.description: Use dbt transformation patterns to organize analytics models, add tests and documentation, create macros, and design incremental processing strategies.
skill.summary: Build organized, tested, and documented dbt transformation projects.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Markdown backticks, dbt model names, and documentation links as execution, cryptography, reconnaissance, and network risks. Review found a single Markdown guide with dbt examples, user-run CLI commands, and official resource links; no hidden execution, exfiltration, prompt injection, or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command alerts point to Markdown fences, inline code spans, SQL examples, YAML examples, and dbt CLI examples. The file is documentation and does not execute these commands automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is inside Markdown code fences or inline formatting in SKILL.md. No runnable script, shell interpolation, or automatic command execution path exists in the skill.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Text Matches
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts match ordinary dbt documentation text such as model descriptions, customer identifiers, documentation guidance, and dbt utility examples. No cryptographic API or hashing implementation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain prose, YAML descriptions, and dbt model names. I found no MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar cryptographic operation in the file.

low_findings.title: Documentation Links Are Benign External References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are resource links to dbt documentation, dbt best practices, dbt-utils, and dbt Discourse. They are not fetched by the skill and do not transmit local data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear only in a Resources section as Markdown links. There is no network client, webhook, credential handling, or data upload logic.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Alerts Are SQL and dbt Terms
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance alerts are caused by benign terms such as id fields, joins, model names, and dbt commands. They do not inspect the host system or collect environment details.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are schema examples and SQL joins in a dbt tutorial. I found no host probing, directory enumeration, process inspection, or secret discovery behavior.

content.value_statement: dbt projects often become hard to test, document, and scale. This skill gives reusable patterns for model layers, tests, docs, macros, and incremental builds.
actual_capabilities: Explains staging, intermediate, and marts model layers for dbt projects.
Provides naming conventions for staging models, dimensions, and fact tables.
Shows patterns for source freshness, model tests, and column documentation.
Outlines macros for reusable SQL and environment-specific development filters.
Compares incremental strategies such as merge, delete-insert, and insert overwrite.
Lists common dbt commands for development, testing, documentation, and debugging.
limitations: It is a guidance-only skill and does not run dbt commands automatically.
Examples use Stripe and Shopify sample domains that may need adaptation.
Warehouse-specific SQL syntax may require changes for your platform.
It does not replace review of data contracts, permissions, or production costs.

use_cases.title: Structure a New dbt Project
use_cases.description: Define model folders, naming conventions, and materialization defaults before building transformations.

use_cases.title: Improve Data Quality Coverage
use_cases.description: Add source freshness, uniqueness, not-null, relationship, recency, and accepted-value tests to important models.

use_cases.title: Optimize Large Transformations
use_cases.description: Choose incremental patterns and development filters for large tables that are expensive to rebuild.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a dbt Folder Structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the dbt transformation patterns skill to design a project structure for my sources, staging models, intermediate logic, and marts. Include naming conventions and where documentation files belong.

prompt_templates.title: Design Tests for Models
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the dbt transformation patterns skill to recommend tests for these dbt models: [list models and key columns]. Include source freshness, primary key checks, relationships, and accepted values.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor Repeated SQL
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the dbt transformation patterns skill to identify repeated transformation logic in my models and propose dbt macros or intermediate models. Explain the tradeoffs for readability and lineage.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Incremental Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the dbt transformation patterns skill to select an incremental strategy for a large fact table. Consider late-arriving data, partitioning, unique keys, schema changes, and full-refresh behavior.

output_examples.input: I need a dbt layout for payments, customers, and orders.
output: A staged project plan with source definitions, staging models, intermediate payment summaries, and core marts.
Suggested names for customer dimensions, order facts, and supporting documentation files.
A short test plan covering keys, relationships, freshness, and accepted values.

output_examples.input: My order fact table is too slow to rebuild every day.
output: A recommendation to evaluate merge or partition overwrite based on late updates and table partitioning.
Guidance for unique keys, lookback windows, schema change settings, and full-refresh safeguards.
Development filter advice to keep local runs small while preserving production behavior.

output_examples.input: We have repeated cents-to-dollars calculations across models.
output: A proposal to move repeated conversion logic into a dbt macro.
Placement guidance for macro files and examples of where the macro should be called.
Testing and documentation reminders so the abstraction remains clear to analysts.

best_practices: Start every source with a staging model that cleans names, types, and metadata once.
Add tests and descriptions near the models they protect so lineage stays understandable.
Use incremental models only when data volume, refresh time, and late updates justify the added complexity.
anti_patterns: Do not build marts directly from raw sources when reusable staging models are missing.
Do not copy the same business logic across many models instead of extracting macros or intermediate models.
Do not rely on incremental builds without a clear full-refresh and backfill strategy.

faq.question: Does this skill execute dbt commands?
faq.answer: No. It provides examples and guidance. Users must run any dbt commands in their own environment.

faq.question: Which dbt project layers does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers sources, staging, intermediate models, marts, macros, tests, documentation, and incremental models.

faq.question: Can it help with dbt tests?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains common tests for uniqueness, not-null values, relationships, freshness, recency, and accepted values.

faq.question: Does it support warehouse-specific SQL?
faq.answer: It gives general dbt SQL patterns. You should adjust date functions and incremental settings for your warehouse.

faq.question: Is it useful for existing dbt projects?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide refactoring, documentation improvements, test coverage reviews, and incremental model decisions.

faq.question: What tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
